cards.title: 1. PACKS
cards._legacyHtml:
Hydrotherapy Packs — Principle
Packs use wet cloths or mud applied to specific body regions. The thermal, mechanical, and reflex effects stimulate circulation, reduce inflammation, and promote healing. Always assess skin integrity and temperature sensitivity before application.
Pack TypeAreaTemperatureDurationIndication
Abdominal PackNavel to pubic regionCold/cool20–40 minConstipation, IBS, hyperacidity, colitis, fever
Chest PackThorax anterior & posteriorCold then warm20–30 minBronchitis, asthma, pneumonia, pleurisy
Gluteal Hip (GH) PackGluteal region & hipsHot or alternate20–30 minSciatica, pelvic congestion, haemorrhoids, LBP
Kidney PackLumbar/flank regionHot20–30 minRenal colic, UTI (adjunct), loin pain, nephritis
Trunk PackAxilla to iliac crestCold45–60 minHigh fever, obesity, chronic toxaemia, insomnia
Throat PackNeck / throatCold20–30 minTonsillitis, pharyngitis, thyroid conditions, sore throat
Wet Girdle PackWaist to mid-thighCold (wrung tight)45–90 min (overnight)Pelvic diseases, constipation, UTI, digestive disorders
Leg PackBoth legs — knee to ankleCold20–30 minVaricose veins, oedema, peripheral neuropathy, tired legs
Head PackForehead / scalpCold20–30 minHeadache, fever, hypertension, sinusitis, eye strain
Towel PackFull body — neck to anklesCold, then warm30–60 minHigh fever, systemic toxaemia, insomnia, detox
Knee PackKnee jointCold or alternating20–30 minOsteoarthritis, knee effusion, gout, bursitis
Full Wet Sheet PackEntire bodyCold (3 stages)Stage 1: 5–15 min · Stage 2: 15–30 min · Stage 3: 30–60 minFever, chronic rheumatism, arthritis, detoxification, insomnia
🟡 Full Wet Sheet Pack — 3 Stages
Stage 1 (Tonic/Cold): Shivering, skin cold, vasoconstriction — duration 5–15 min. Stage 2 (Neutral/Warming): Patient feels comfortable, skin warm — 15–30 min. Stage 3 (Heating/Diaphoretic): Profuse perspiration, detox effect — 30–60 min. Always have attendant present. CI: severe cardiac disease, advanced age without monitoring, open wounds.

cards.title: 2. BATHS
cards._legacyHtml:
Hydrotherapy Baths — Classification
Baths use immersion or targeted water application for circulatory, neurological, and metabolic effects. Temperature and duration are key therapeutic variables.
Bath TypeDescription / TechniqueTemp / DurationIndication
Hip Bath — ColdPelvis immersed, legs & shoulders out; friction of abdomen10–18°C · 10–20 minConstipation, piles, impotence, sexual disorders, prostatitis
Hip Bath — HotSame position; hot water with cool cloth on head40–45°C · 10–20 minDysmenorrhoea, cystitis, ovarian pain, pelvic congestion
Hip Bath — NeutralTepid immersion, relaxing effect on nervous system33–36°C · 20–30 minAnxiety, insomnia, neurological disorders, psychosomatic
Hip Bath — Alternating (Contrast)Hot 3 min → Cold 1 min, repeat 3–4 cycles; end coldHot 40–45°C, Cold 10–15°CChronic pelvic inflammation, endometriosis, uterine fibroids
Foot Bath — ColdBoth feet immersed to ankle level10–18°C · 5–10 minHeadache, epistaxis, varicose veins, cold feet, insomnia
Foot Bath — HotFeet immersed; add mustard powder for bronchitis40–44°C · 15–20 minCold & flu, bronchitis, URI, headache, poor circulation
Foot Bath — AlternatingHot 3 min → Cold 1 min, 3–4 cycles, end coldHot 40–44°C, Cold 10–15°CPoor circulation, varicose veins, peripheral neuropathy
Sitz Bath — ColdSitting in shallow tub; only hips/perineum immersed10–18°C · 10–20 minConstipation, haemorrhoids, uterine prolapse, impotence
Sitz Bath — HotAs above, hot water40–45°C · 10–15 minDysmenorrhoea, cystitis, anal fissure, perineal pain
Spinal BathNarrow trough; spine submerged, head & legs outCold 18–24°C or Neutral 33–36°C · 20–40 minHypertension, nervous disorders, insomnia, spinal conditions, anxiety
Full Immersion Bath — ColdComplete body immersion except head10–15°C · 5–10 minHigh fever (>39°C), heat exhaustion, acute inflammatory disorders
Full Immersion Bath — HotComplete immersion; cool cloth on head; close supervision40–44°C · 10–15 minChronic arthritis, muscle spasm, chronic toxaemia, cold extremities
Full Immersion Bath — NeutralExtended neutral immersion; highly sedating33–36°C · 20–60 minInsomnia, anxiety, psychosis, agitation, skin conditions (psoriasis)
Contrast Bath (Limbs)Alternating hot and cold immersion of limbsHot 40–44°C 3 min, Cold 10–15°C 1 min, 4–6 cyclesArthritis, sprains, peripheral vascular disease, fatigue, CRPS
Jet / Douche BathHigh-pressure water jet applied to body regionsCold, warm, or alternating · 5–15 minMuscle fatigue, obesity, cellulite, poor circulation, lymphoedema
Whirlpool BathTurbulent water immersion with air jets for massage36–40°C · 15–20 minArthritis, chronic pain, wound healing, sports injuries, PVD
Jacuzzi BathHot tub with air/water jets; full body hydromassage37–40°C · 15–20 minRelaxation, chronic musculoskeletal pain, stress, insomnia
SaunaDry heat (Finnish style); temperature 80–100°C; low humidity80–100°C dry · 10–20 min + cold plungeDetoxification, chronic pain, CVD prevention (evidence grade B), stress
Steam BathMoist heat; humidity ~100%; full body except head43–49°C moist · 10–20 minRespiratory disorders, obesity, arthritis, detox, chronic skin conditions
Steam InhalationSteam inhaled through towel tent; add tulsi/eucalyptus~50°C steam · 10–15 minURI, bronchitis, sinusitis, asthma (adjunct), nasal congestion
Head Bath (Shirodhara style)Continuous stream of warm water/oil over forehead36–38°C · 20–40 minHeadache, insomnia, anxiety, scalp disorders, hypertension
Arm BathArms immersed to elbow in hot/cold waterVariable · 10–15 minCardiac reflex therapy, hypertension, shoulder-arm pain, angina
Eye BathEyes immersed in eyecup with saline or boric solutionCool/lukewarm · 5–10 minEye strain, non-infective conjunctivitis, eye fatigue, dryness
Peat/Mud BathFull body immersion in therapeutic peat or heated mud38–42°C · 20–30 minRheumatoid arthritis, osteoarthritis, chronic pain, skin diseases
Salt BathConcentrated salt (Dead Sea / Epsom) in full bath37–40°C · 20 minPsoriasis, eczema, muscle soreness, fibromyalgia, dermatitis
Oat BathColloidal oatmeal added to bath waterLukewarm · 15–20 minEczema, pruritus, chickenpox, atopic dermatitis, dry skin
Medicated Herbal BathHerbal decoctions added to bath (neem, turmeric, dashamoola)37–40°C · 20–30 minSkin infections, fungal conditions, joint pain, post-fever recovery
Vaginal DoucheWarm douche solution (saline, boric, antiseptic) directed internally37–40°C · 10–15 minVaginal infections (adjunct), leucorrhoea, cervicitis — requires strict sterility
🟡 Temperature Safety Guide
Very cold: <18°C | Cold: 18–26°C | Cool: 26–33°C | Neutral: 33–36°C | Tepid: 36–37°C | Warm: 37–40°C | Hot: 40–45°C | Very hot: >45°C (use with extreme caution). Always test with elbow before patient immersion. Never leave elderly, children, or neurologically impaired patients unattended in baths.

cards.title: 3. TREATMENTS
cards._legacyHtml:
Hydrotherapy Treatments — Special Procedures
These procedures use water in specific internal or external applications beyond immersion baths and packs.
TreatmentTechniqueIndicationKey Notes
EnemaWarm water (plain, herbal, coffee, or medicated) introduced rectally via enema can (1–1.5 L); retain 5–15 minConstipation, bowel cleansing, detox, before proceduresUse lukewarm water (36–38°C); left lateral position; max 1.5L; check for rectal/colonic contraindications first
Colon Hydrotherapy (Colonic)Closed-system device delivers warm filtered water into colon via rectal catheter; multiple fills/releases under supervisionChronic constipation, IBS-C, detoxification, pre-colonoscopy (institutional only)Only by trained therapist; avoid in IBD, diverticulitis, CRC, post-surgery, pregnancy; monitor electrolytes
Oil Injection (Rectal)Warm sesame/olive oil (100–150 ml) administered per rectum; retain overnightSevere constipation, dry stool impaction, haemorrhoids (lubrication), anal fissureWarm oil to body temp; avoid if active rectal bleeding; oil provides lubrication without stimulating peristalsis
FomentationHot wet cloths (flannel dipped in boiling water, wrung out) applied to body region; change every 3–5 min × 3 cycles; finish with cold frictionChest congestion, abdominal cramping, muscle spasm, pleuritis, pericarditis, renal colicAlways test temperature before application; protect bony prominences; end each session with cold rub for reactive hyperaemia
Ice Application / CryotherapyIce pack, ice massage, or crushed ice in towel applied to acute injury or inflamed area; 10–15 min on / 10 offAcute sports injuries, sprains, burns (first aid), acute joint inflammation, febrile convulsions (head)Never apply ice directly to skin — wrap in cloth; max 15 min continuous; avoid in Raynaud's, PVD, sensory loss areas
Infrared (IR) Lamp TherapyIR lamp positioned 45–60 cm from skin; expose region for 15–30 min; check patient comfort every 5 minChronic musculoskeletal pain, arthritis, sinusitis, wound healing, poor peripheral circulationNever use over acute inflammation, malignancy, areas with sensory deficit, or over metal implants; keep distance ≥45cm; document skin response
Postural DrainageGravity-assisted positioning to drain specific lung segments; combine with percussion/vibration; 15–30 min per sessionBronchiectasis, COPD (mucus clearance), post-operative pulmonary atelectasis, cystic fibrosis (adjunct)Avoid immediately post-meals; monitor SpO₂; stop if haemoptysis, acute asthma attack, or haemodynamic compromise
Vibro Therapy (Mechanical Vibration)Vibrating device applied to back, joint, or muscle group; 10–20 min per areaMuscle fatigue, spasticity, peripheral circulation, lymphoedema, DOMSAvoid over fractures, acute DVT, thrombophlebitis, pacemakers, spinal cord injury (without specialist guidance); document response
🟡 Enema Safety Protocol
Equipment: sterile enema can, tubing, rectal nozzle. Lubricate tip with coconut oil. Patient: left lateral, knee-chest position. Bag height: 60 cm above bed level (not higher — reduces pressure). Water temp: 36–38°C. Volume: 500–1000 ml for cleansing; 100–150 ml for retention enema. Hold: 10–15 min if tolerated. Absolute CI: rectal cancer, ulcerative colitis (active flare), recent colonic surgery, intestinal obstruction, peritonitis.

cards.title: Mud Therapy — Principles & Application Techniques
cards._legacyHtml:
Definition
Mud therapy (Mrittikapachanam) uses therapeutic earth/clay applied externally for its cooling, absorptive, mineral-rich, and anti-inflammatory properties. A fundamental BNYS modality.
Pack TypeAreaThicknessDurationIndication
Local Mud PackAbdomen, eyes, head, knee1.5–2 cm20–30 minConstipation, headache, fever, inflammation
Full Mud BathEntire body (except head)1 cm all over20–45 minSkin diseases, arthritis, obesity, detox
Partial Mud BathArms, legs, or trunk1 cm20–30 minJoint pain, varicose veins, local skin disorders
1
Preparation — Wet therapeutic mud (black cotton soil or fullers earth) to soft-dough consistency. Strain through muslin to remove grit. Spread on cloth or apply directly.
2
Application — Apply in smooth strokes. Cover with thin cloth to retain moisture. Keep patient warm and comfortable. Never apply dry mud.
3
Removal — Remove before mud dries completely. Wipe with damp cloth, then warm water. Pat dry — do not rub. Allow 15–20 min rest after.
🟡 Abdominal Mud Pack — Most prescribed
Apply cool mud pack from navel to pubic symphysis. Duration: 20–30 min morning before breakfast. Course: daily × 14 days for chronic conditions. Indications: constipation, IBS, hyperacidity, colitis, liver disorders. The cooling effect ↓ intestinal inflammation, ↑ peristalsis, and calms vagal tone. Combine with hip bath for enhanced effect.

cards.title: Eye Mud Pack & Special Applications
cards._legacyHtml:
Eye Mud Pack Indications
Eye strain, non-infective conjunctivitis, headache from eye strain, dark circles, periorbital puffiness.
1
Preparation — Use only fine, grit-free, triple-filtered mud. Shape into two oval pads (5×3 cm, 1 cm thick) on gauze strips. Refrigerate for 10 min for enhanced cooling effect in inflammation.
2
Application — Patient supine, eyes closed. Place gently over closed eyelids — zero pressure on eyeball. Duration: 10–20 minutes.
3
Removal — Remove gently. Eyes closed 2 more min. Splash cool clean water over closed eyes. Pat dry.
Eye Pack Contraindications
Acute infective conjunctivitis · corneal ulcer · open wounds around eyes · known eye allergies · recent eye surgery
🟡 Course for Refractive Errors
Daily morning application × 21 days, then alternate days × 1 month. Combine with palming exercises and screen reduction. Never use mud with any visible grit near eyes.

cards.title: Mud Therapy — Contraindications & Safety
cards._legacyHtml:
Absolute Contraindications
ConditionReason
Open wounds, ulcers, broken skinInfection risk — therapeutic mud is NOT sterile
Active skin infections (fungal/bacterial)May spread infection
Severe eczema/psoriasis (acute flare)May irritate broken/inflamed skin
High fever (>103°F / 39.4°C)Full mud bath may cause collapse
Severe cardiac or renal failureCirculatory stress from full body application
Pregnancy (abdominal application)Pressure contraindicated
Known clay/mineral allergyRisk of contact dermatitis
Precautions
Patch test 24h before first application. Warm patient before and after in cold climates. Never apply over varicose veins with broken skin. Elderly and debilitated: shorter duration, constant monitoring. Always document skin response pre and post application.

cards.title: Massage Therapy — Principles & Basic Strokes
cards._legacyHtml:
Massage Therapy in BNYS
Massage uses systematic manipulation of soft tissues (skin, muscles, fascia) to promote circulation, reduce tension, enhance lymphatic drainage, and stimulate the autonomic nervous system. Core modality in BNYS clinical practice.
StrokeSanskrit / TermTechniqueEffect
EffleurageStrokingLong, gliding strokes in direction of venous/lymph flow; whole hand or thumbs; light→deepWarms tissue, promotes venous return, relaxes, introduces patient to touch
PetrissageKneadingPicking up and squeezing muscle bulk; alternate hands in circular motion↑ Muscle circulation, removes metabolic waste, reduces muscle tone
FrictionDeep FrictionSmall circular movements with fingertips; deepest pressure; transverse or circularBreaks adhesions, scar tissue remodelling, deep joint/tendon therapy (Cyriax)
TapotementPercussionHacking, cupping, clapping, beating, tapping — rhythmic percussive strokesStimulates circulation, loosens mucus (chest), tones muscles, reflex stimulation
VibrationTremblingFine, rapid oscillatory movement transmitted through hands to tissueSedates nervous system, loosens chest secretions, relieves spasm
🟡 General Body Massage Sequence
Begin supine: effleurage × 3 strokes legs → trunk → arms. Then: petrissage major muscle groups. Then: friction over tight areas. Then: tapotement. End: effleurage. Turn prone: repeat sequence on back. Duration: 45–60 min full body. Oil: sesame (warming), coconut (cooling), olive (anti-inflammatory). Always work toward the heart.

cards.title: Massage Types & Specialised Techniques
cards._legacyHtml:
TypeKey TechniqueIndicationContraindication
Swedish MassageAll 5 classic strokes; full body; 60 minGeneral relaxation, stress, muscle tension, improved circulationAcute DVT, open wounds, fever, severe osteoporosis
Deep Tissue MassageSlow strokes with deep pressure on specific fascial layersChronic muscle pain, postural issues, fibromyalgia, adhesionsBleeding disorders, anticoagulants, acute injury, malignancy over site
Lymphatic Drainage (MLD)Very light pressure (40 mmHg); slow; stimulates lymphatic vesselsLymphoedema ★, post-surgical oedema, fibromyalgia, immune supportActive infection, active malignancy (without oncology clearance), CHF, DVT
Abdominal MassageClockwise circular strokes following colon direction; 10–15 minConstipation ★, IBS-C, bowel motility disorders, bloating, digestive dysfunctionAcute appendicitis, intestinal obstruction, active IBD flare, pregnancy (third trimester)
Foot ReflexologySpecific pressure points on soles and toes corresponding to body organsStress-related conditions, organ support, insomnia, headache, general wellnessActive foot infection, DVT of legs, severe varicose veins, fractures
Chest Physiotherapy (CPT)Tapotement + vibration on chest segments + postural drainage positionsBronchiectasis, COPD, cystic fibrosis, post-operative atelectasisHaemoptysis, rib fractures, severe osteoporosis, acute MI
🟡 BNYS Therapeutic Oil Massage Protocol
Morning massage before bath: 45–60 min. Oil selection: Sesame (Tila) = warming, Vatahara — best for most conditions. Coconut = cooling, Pittahara — for fever, inflammation. Dashamoola taila = joint pain. Bhringamalakadi = hair/scalp. Technique: long strokes with 5–10 ml warm oil. Post-massage: warm bath/shower. For elderly/debilitated: reduce pressure, focus on legs and back only.

cards.title: Massage Contraindications & Safety Guidelines
cards._legacyHtml:
Absolute Contraindications
ConditionReason
Acute DVT / thrombophlebitisRisk of embolism — potentially fatal
Active malignancy (at site)Risk of dissemination — requires oncology clearance
Active skin infections, open wounds, burnsCross-contamination, pain, tissue damage
Acute fractures, dislocationsMechanical injury risk
Severe bleeding disorders / on anticoagulantsHaematoma, internal bleeding risk
High fever (>38.5°C)Increased metabolic demand, risk of spread of infection
First trimester of pregnancyAvoid abdomen; deep pressure contraindicated throughout
Precautions
Always obtain informed consent. Drape patient appropriately. Avoid direct pressure on varicose veins. Monitor for signs of discomfort throughout. Wash hands before and after. Document type, area, oil used, duration, and patient response after every session.

cards.title: Fasting Therapy — Principles & Types
cards._legacyHtml:
Therapeutic Fasting — Core BNYS Principle
Fasting (Upavasa) is the voluntary abstinence from some or all food and drink for a defined period. It triggers autophagy, reduces inflammation, improves insulin sensitivity, and activates the healing power of nature. Must be medically supervised in clinical settings.
Fasting TypeProtocolDurationIndication
Total Fast (Water-only)Water only (2–3 L/day); rest; strict monitoring1–3 days (clinical); up to 7–10 days only in specialized centres with daily monitoringChronic disease detox, autoimmune conditions, resistant obesity; requires physician supervision
Juice FastingFresh vegetable/fruit juices (200–300 ml q4h); no solid food; herbal teas3–21 daysDetoxification, obesity, metabolic syndrome, T2DM reversal, chronic inflammation
Fruit Fast / Mono DietSingle fruit or seasonal fruit only (e.g., watermelon, grapes, apple)3–7 daysTransitional fast, mild detox, constipation, hyperacidity, spring cleanse
Intermittent Fasting — 16:816h fasting window + 8h eating window daily; water/black coffee allowed in fastOngoing lifestyle protocolObesity, metabolic syndrome, T2DM, NAFLD, longevity; Grade A evidence for weight loss
Intermittent Fasting — 5:25 days normal eating + 2 non-consecutive days of ≤500 kcal intakeOngoing protocolWeight management, metabolic improvement, cardiovascular risk reduction
Ekadashi FastComplete fast on 11th lunar day (twice monthly); religious + therapeutic basis24h × twice monthlyDigestive rest, metabolic reset, spiritual practice in BNYS context
Partial Fast / Restricted DietElimination of specific food groups (gluten, dairy, sugar); caloric restriction (1200–1500 kcal)Weeks to monthsAutoimmune conditions, food sensitivity, weight management, lifestyle diseases
🟡 Fasting — Biochemical Cascade
Hours 0–8: glycogen depleted. Hours 8–16: ↑ gluconeogenesis, ↑ fat oxidation. Hours 16–24: ↑ ketone bodies, ↑ autophagy (Nobel 2016 — Ohsumi). Hours 24–72: full ketosis, ↑ BDNF, ↑ IGF-1 sensitivity, deep cellular repair. Breaking fast: always with small portions of easily digestible food (fruit/vegetable broth). Never break a long fast with a heavy meal.

cards.title: Fasting — Monitoring, Contraindications & Refeeding
cards._legacyHtml:
⚠️ Fasting Monitoring — Daily Parameters
ParameterFrequencyAction if Abnormal
Blood glucoseTwice daily (fasting + 2h PM)BG <3.5 mmol/L → break fast immediately with juice
Blood pressure & pulseTwice dailyPostural drop >20 mmHg or HR >100 → oral rehydration, reassess
Body weightDaily (same time)Loss >1 kg/day → increase fluid intake; investigate
Urine output & colourObserve each voidDark urine → ↑ water intake; anuria → break fast, refer
Electrolytes (Na⁺, K⁺)Every 3 days for total fast >3 daysK⁺ <3.0 or Na⁺ <130 → supplement and reassess
Ketones (urine/blood)Daily during water fastMild ketonuria = expected; vomiting + high ketones → break fast
Absolute Contraindications to Therapeutic Fasting
T1DM · Active eating disorder (anorexia/bulimia) · Pregnancy or breastfeeding · Severe underweight (BMI <18) · Active malignancy without specialist clearance · Severe cardiac failure or arrhythmia · Active TB or sepsis · Children under 12 years
🟡 Refeeding Protocol
After 3+ days of total/juice fast: Day 1 of refeeding: diluted vegetable broth (200 ml q4h). Day 2: fresh fruit + vegetable juice. Day 3: soft fruits, cooked vegetables, mung dal soup. Day 4+: gradual return to full diet. Refeeding syndrome risk in prolonged fasting: ↓ PO₄, ↓ Mg, ↓ K — monitor electrolytes in first 48h of refeeding. Never return directly to normal diet after >5 days of fasting.

cards.title: Fasting — Evidence Base & BNYS Protocols
cards._legacyHtml:
ConditionEvidenceRecommended ProtocolDuration
Obesity / Metabolic SyndromeGrade A — multiple RCTs for IF protocols16:8 TRE or 5:2 protocol with Mediterranean diet breakMinimum 12 weeks for measurable benefit
T2DM (non-insulin dependent)Grade B — supervised fasting shows HbA1c ↓ 0.9–1.4%Juice fast 7–14 days + dietary modification follow-up; monitor BG closelyInstitutional setting only; GP co-management
Rheumatoid ArthritisGrade B — modified Buchinger fasting trials5–7 day supervised modified fast (250–500 kcal) + Mediterranean diet1–2 ×/year in remission phase only
Hypertension (Stage 1–2)Grade B — water fasting RCTs (Goldhamer et al.)10–14 day medically supervised water fast in resistant HTN; average ↓ 37/13 mmHgInpatient setting; daily monitoring required
Chronic Constipation / IBS-CGrade C — clinical experienceJuice fast 3–5 days + progressive elimination dietOutpatient, weekly review
🟡 BNYS Fasting Assessment Protocol
Pre-fasting workup: FBC, metabolic panel (Na/K/glucose/creatinine/LFT), HbA1c, ECG, BP. Medications review — must taper or suspend: metformin (lactic acidosis risk), antihypertensives (hypotension risk), sulphonylureas (hypoglycaemia). Supplements allowed during juice fast: electrolyte solution, magnesium glycinate, vitamin C, B12. Stop: anticoagulants require haematology review before any prolonged fast.

cards.title: Chromotherapy (Colour Therapy)
cards._legacyHtml:
Chromotherapy — Principle
Chromotherapy uses specific wavelengths of visible light to influence physiological and psychological states. Based on the theory that different colours carry different energetic frequencies that interact with the body's energy fields and organ systems.
ColourWavelengthPhysiological EffectIndication
Red620–750 nmStimulates, warms, increases circulation and energyAnaemia, depression, chronic fatigue, hypothyroidism, paralysis
Orange590–620 nmStimulates respiratory system, anti-spasmodicBronchitis, asthma, kidney stones, arthritis, hypothyroidism
Yellow570–590 nmStimulates nervous system and lymphatic system, laxativeConstipation, diabetes, liver disorders, skin conditions, obesity
Green495–570 nmBalancing, neutral, calming, antiseptic via lightHypertension, cardiac conditions, ulcers, headache, nervous exhaustion
Blue450–495 nmCooling, sedating, anti-inflammatory, reduces painFever ★, headache, insomnia, throat infections, hyperthyroidism, burns
Indigo420–450 nmDeep sedation, anti-inflammatoryMental disorders, eye conditions, ear problems, haemostasis
Violet380–420 nmCalming, purifying, suppresses appetiteAnxiety, psychosis, neurological conditions, scalp disorders
🟡 Chromotherapy Techniques
Lamp method: coloured glass filter over lamp, exposed to body at 30–60 cm for 15–30 min. Solarised water: water exposed to coloured glass bottle in sunlight for 4–6 h — drink 100 ml before meals. Colour breathing: visualisation technique. Light box: full-spectrum for SAD. Duration: 5–7 sessions for acute; 21 sessions for chronic conditions. Clinical evidence for chromotherapy is Grade C — adjunctive use only. Strong Grade A evidence exists for phototherapy (UVB) in psoriasis and SAD (white light therapy).

cards.title: Magneto Therapy
cards._legacyHtml:
Magneto Therapy — Principle
Magneto therapy uses permanent magnets or pulsed electromagnetic fields (PEMF) to influence biological systems. Magnetic fields interact with ionic charges in tissues, alter enzyme kinetics, and modulate pain pathways.
TypeTechniqueIndicationEvidence
Static Magnets (permanent)North pole (negative) = sedating; South pole (positive) = stimulating. Apply over affected area for 30–60 min.Pain (joint, headache), insomnia, hypertensionGrade C — limited RCT evidence; placebo effect likely significant
PEMF (Pulsed EMF)Device delivers programmed EM pulses; 20–60 min/dayNon-union fractures (FDA approved ★★), osteoporosis, chronic pain, cartilage repairGrade A for non-union fractures; Grade B for OA pain; Grade B for depression (rTMS variant)
Magnetic WaterWater passed through magnetic device; drunk as solarised waterRenal calculi, UTI, general wellnessGrade D — theoretical only; no robust evidence
🟡 Safety — Absolute Contraindications for Magnets
Absolute CI: Pacemakers ★★★ (pacemaker may be inhibited by strong magnets — potentially fatal). Cochlear implants. Metallic implants in treatment area. Pregnancy (avoid over gravid uterus). Active malignancy. Precautions: avoid use over eyes. Do not combine strong magnets with other ferromagnetic objects. South pole (positive) stimulates — use cautiously near malignancy sites.

cards.title: Acupressure & Marma Therapy
cards._legacyHtml:
Acupressure — Principle
Acupressure applies manual pressure to specific anatomical points (acupoints/marma points) to unblock Qi/prana flow, stimulate nerve reflexes, and modulate neurohumoral pathways. Evidence supports effects via endorphin release, serotonin modulation, and vagal activation.
Key PointLocationIndicationTechnique
PC-6 (Neiguan)2 cun proximal to wrist crease, between flexor tendonsNausea ★★★ (CINV, morning sickness, post-op), anxiety, palpitationsFirm pressure thumb × 1 min, bilateral; Sea-Band wristbands for CINV
ST-36 (Zusanli)3 cun below patella, 1 cun lateral to tibial crestDigestive disorders ★★, immunity, fatigue, knee pain, T2DM adjunctFirm thumb pressure 60–90 sec, stimulate once daily
LI-4 (Hegu)Dorsum of hand, between 1st and 2nd metacarpalsHeadache ★★, pain (general), constipation, dental pain, feverFirm thumb pressure × 1 min; AVOID in pregnancy (labour stimulant)
LV-3 (Taichong)Dorsum of foot, between 1st and 2nd metatarsalsStress, headache, liver disorders, menstrual pain, insomniaFirm pressure × 60 sec; may be tender in liver Qi stagnation
SP-6 (Sanyinjiao)3 cun above medial malleolus, posterior to tibiaMenstrual disorders ★, insomnia, digestive, oedema, urinaryFirm pressure × 60 sec; AVOID in pregnancy (strong uterine stimulant)
KD-1 (Yongquan)Junction of anterior 1/3 and posterior 2/3 of soleHypertension, anxiety, insomnia, headache, groundingCircular pressure × 2 min; effective at bedtime for insomnia
🟡 CINV Acupressure Protocol (Grade A Evidence)
PC-6 stimulation: apply firm circular pressure with thumb for 3 min, 3× daily for 5 days post-chemotherapy. Combined ST-36 stimulation adds digestive benefit. Sea-Band acupressure bands worn continuously during chemo cycle. ESMO 2024 guidelines list acupressure as a Grade A complementary recommendation for CINV prevention. Mechanism: PC-6 → reduces gastric dysrhythmia via vagal modulation + ↑ serotonin at 5-HT3 receptors.

cards.title: Naturopathic Wellness — Chromopuncture, Magnets & Lifestyle Integration
cards._legacyHtml:
Integrating Other Modalities in BNYS Practice
BNYS uses a wide spectrum of non-pharmacological modalities. All are best used as adjuncts within a comprehensive naturopathic management plan that includes diet, yoga, hydrotherapy, and lifestyle modification as the foundation.
ModalityMechanismBest Evidence UseGrade
Chromotherapy (Blue light)Melatonin suppression via retinal receptors; circadian phase shiftingSAD (Seasonal Affective Disorder) ★★★, circadian rhythm disordersA
PEMF TherapyElectromagnetic induction alters calcium channels, ↑ NO, ↓ inflammatory cytokinesNon-union fractures ★★★, knee OA, depression (rTMS)A (fractures), B (OA)
AcupressureEndorphin release, vagal modulation, serotonin at PC-6CINV ★★★, low back pain, dysmenorrhoea, labour painA (CINV), B (LBP)
Chromotherapy (General)Psychoneuroimmunological response to wavelength-specific lightAdjunct for stress, mood, sleep — no standalone Grade A evidenceC
Static Magnetic TherapyIon transport modulation; theoretical pain gate mechanismNo reliable evidence for clinical benefit over placebo in most conditionsD
🟡 Naturopathic Therapeutic Order — When to Use These Modalities
In BNYS the therapeutic order: 1) Remove obstacles to cure (diet, stress, toxins). 2) Stimulate healing power (diet, exercise, sleep). 3) Support weakened organs (herbal, nutrition). 4) Address pathology — hydrotherapy, mud, massage, acupressure. 5) Pharmacological/surgical (refer). Chromotherapy, magnets, and acupressure sit at Level 4. They should never replace Levels 1–3. Always document patient response to guide continuation of any Level 4 modality.

cards.title: Ayurveda in BNYS Practice — Overview
cards._legacyHtml:
Ayurveda Integration in BNYS
Ayurveda (Science of Life) is India's oldest codified natural medical system. BNYS practitioners integrate Ayurvedic principles for constitution assessment (Prakriti), dietary guidance (Ahara), lifestyle (Vihara), and Panchakarma therapies. Overlap with naturopathy is significant in philosophy and clinical application.
ConceptDescriptionClinical Relevance
TridoshaVata (air/ether), Pitta (fire/water), Kapha (earth/water) — constitutional energiesGuides dietary, herbal, and lifestyle prescriptions individualised to patient constitution
Prakriti AssessmentConstitution determined by pulse (Nadi Pariksha), physical, mental, and metabolic characteristicsFoundation for personalised naturopathic treatment planning
PanchakarmaFive classical purification procedures: Vamana, Virechana, Basti, Nasya, RaktamokshanaDeep detoxification protocols — require Ayurvedic physician oversight; BNYS may assist with preparatory and post-treatment phases
Dinacharya (Daily Routine)Oil pulling (Gandusha), tongue scraping, Abhyangam, yoga, meditation, early risingEvidence-based lifestyle medicine supported by circadian biology and microbiome research
Rasayana (Rejuvenation)Adaptogens and rejuvenating herbs: Ashwagandha ★, Shatavari, Amalaki, Brahmi, GuduchiGrade B–A evidence for stress, cognitive function, immunity, and longevity markers
🟡 Prakriti Types — Quick Reference
Vata: thin, light, dry skin, creative, anxious, variable appetite, prone to constipation, insomnia, joint pain. Pitta: medium build, oily/warm skin, sharp intellect, angry, strong appetite, prone to acidity, inflammation, skin rashes. Kapha: heavy, cool/oily skin, calm, slow metabolism, prone to obesity, diabetes, respiratory congestion. Most individuals are dual-doshic (e.g., Vata-Pitta). Treat the dominant vitiated dosha (Vikriti), not baseline Prakriti.

cards.title: Abhyangam & Kairali Massage
cards._legacyHtml:
Abhyangam — Ayurvedic Full Body Oil Massage
Abhyangam is the cornerstone of Ayurvedic Panchakarma. Warm medicated oil applied in downward strokes to nourish skin, joints, and nervous system. Documented effects: ↓ cortisol, ↑ HRV, ↑ skin integrity, improved sleep quality.
1
Oil Selection — Vata: warm sesame or Dhanwantaram tailam. Pitta: coconut or Brahmi tailam. Kapha: dry garshana (silk gloves) or light mustard oil. General: Kshirabala tailam (750) for nervous system; Mahanarayan tailam for musculoskeletal.
2
Technique — Warm oil to body temperature. Long strokes (downward, toward feet) on limbs. Circular strokes on joints. Gentle circular strokes on abdomen (clockwise). Duration: 45–60 min. Two therapists (Dwi-hasta) in classical setting.
3
Post-treatment — Steam therapy (Swedana) for 10–15 min after Abhyangam to allow oil penetration. Then warm herbal bath. Rest for 30 min. Avoid cold, exertion, and heavy food for 2 hours.
🟡 Kairali Ayurvedic Treatments
Kairali is a Kerala-based Ayurvedic tradition emphasising authentic Panchakarma. Key treatments: Pizhichil (oil bath — warm oil poured continuously over body × 60–90 min); Njavara Kizhi (rice bolus massage in herbal decoction — for neurological conditions); Elakizhi (herbal leaf bolus massage for musculoskeletal pain). These require trained Panchakarma therapists and appropriate clinical setting.

cards.title: Shirodhara & Kshirodhara
cards._legacyHtml:
Shirodhara — Continuous Oil/Liquid Stream on Forehead
A continuous, thin stream of warm oil (or medicated decoction) poured over the Ajna chakra region (mid-forehead) for 30–60 minutes. Profoundly relaxing. Documented neurological and HPA axis effects.
VariantFluid UsedTemperatureIndication
Tailadhara / Classic ShirodharaSesame oil, Brahmi tailam, Kshirabala tailam37–40°CInsomnia ★★, anxiety, hypertension, stress-related disorders, neurological conditions
KshirodharaWarm medicated milk (cow's milk + herbal decoction)37–40°CPitta disorders, burning sensations, migraine, eye disorders, psychosomatic conditions
TakradharaMedicated buttermilk (with Amalaki, Brahmi, etc.)Room temp (cooling)Psoriasis of scalp, dandruff, Pitta imbalance, excess heat, hypertension
KashayadharaHerbal decoction (Dashamoola, etc.)37–40°CVata-type neurological conditions, chronic pain, spasticity
1
Setup — Patient supine on Droni (wooden treatment table). Eyes covered with cotton pads. Steady stream from Dharapatra (clay/brass pot with wick) adjusted to 2–3 cm above forehead. Stream oscillates slowly side to side (2 cm each side) for even coverage.
2
Duration — Minimum 30 min; optimal 45–60 min. Course: 7–14 consecutive days for chronic conditions. Monitor patient throughout for discomfort, hair/eye irritation, or sudden anxiety.
3
Post-treatment — Gentle scalp massage for 5 min. Warm shower. Rest 30 min. Avoid cold water on head for 6 hours. Avoid mental exertion for remainder of day.
🟡 Mechanism of Shirodhara
Shirodhara activates the prefrontal cortex via tactile receptors, ↓ sympathetic tone (↓ NE, ↑ 5-HT), activates the limbic system's relaxation response, and may stimulate the pineal gland via Ajna chakra stimulation. Studies show: ↑ HRV, ↓ cortisol, improved sleep architecture (↑ REM), ↓ GAD-7 scores. CIs: active scalp infection, severe cervical spondylosis, recent head injury, allergy to oil used, recent eye surgery.

cards.title: Tailadhara & Shirolepa
cards._legacyHtml:
Tailadhara — Continuous Oil Bath (Pizhichil)
Pizhichil or Tailadhara involves continuous warm medicated oil poured over the entire body (except head) by two therapists simultaneously for 60–90 minutes. Combined with gentle massage. One of the most nourishing Panchakarma procedures.
TreatmentDescriptionIndicationDuration / Course
PizhichilWarm Kshirabala tailam poured continuously over body; 2 therapists; 2–4 litres oil per sessionHemiplegia, paraplegia, rheumatoid arthritis, neurological degenerative disorders, chronic vata conditions60–90 min/day × 7–21 days
Njavara KizhiBoluses of cooked Njavara rice (in Bala root decoction + milk) used for massage; highly nourishingMuscular wasting, neuromuscular disorders, sports recovery, malnutrition, spastic conditions45–60 min/day × 7–14 days
Shirolepa — Medicated Head Paste Application
Shirolepa involves applying a thick medicated herbal paste (Lepa) to the scalp and hair for therapeutic effect. A cap may be placed over the paste to enhance penetration.
IngredientIndicationDuration
Brahmi + Amalaki pasteAlopecia, premature greying, dandruff, scalp inflammation30–45 min, 3× weekly × 4 weeks
Neem + Turmeric pasteScalp psoriasis, seborrhoeic dermatitis, fungal scalp infection (adjunct)20–30 min, alternate days × 2 weeks
Shatavari + Coconut milk pasteDry hair, stress-related hair loss, scalp nourishment30 min, 2× weekly × 4 weeks
🟡 Pizhichil Protocol — Key Points
Room must be warm (28°C+). Oil pre-warmed to 40–42°C. Two therapists work in synchrony. Patient position: supine, then prone (30 min each), then lateral. Oil must be continuously maintained at therapeutic temperature using a warming vessel. Post-procedure: gentle massage to absorb remaining oil, then Kizhi (bolus) or Nadi Sweda, then herbal bath. Total oil used: 2–4 litres per session (expensive — budget planning needed). Session frequency: daily or alternate days × 7–21 days.

cards.title: Janu Basti, Kati Basti & Greeva Basti
cards._legacyHtml:
Basti (Retention) Treatments — Principle
Basti treatments involve creating a well (using wet black gram dough) over a specific joint or spinal region. Warm medicated oil is filled and retained in the well for 30–45 minutes to deeply nourish the targeted tissue. The term "Basti" in this context means "retention" — distinct from Panchakarma enema Basti.
TreatmentLocationOil UsedDurationIndication
Janu BastiKnee joint — over patellaMahanarayana tailam, Kshirabala tailam (750)30–45 min/day × 7–14 daysKnee OA ★★, knee effusion, ligament injuries, chondromalacia, post-surgical knee rehabilitation
Kati BastiLumbar spine — L1 to S1 regionMahanarayan tailam, Sahacharadi tailam, Dhanwantaram tailam30–45 min/day × 7–14 daysCLBP ★★★, disc herniation (adjunct), sciatica, degenerative disc disease, lumbar spondylosis
Greeva BastiCervical spine — C1–T1 regionKshirabala tailam, Dhanwantaram tailam30–45 min/day × 7–14 daysCervical spondylosis ★★, cervical disc disease, stiff neck, tension headache, brachialgia
Hridaya BastiCardiac region — sternum/precordiumArjuna Ksheerapaka (medicated milk), sesame oil30–40 min × 7 daysCardiac weakness (adjunct), anxiety, chest tightness, early cardiomyopathy (integrative only)
Nabhi BastiNavel regionWarm sesame oil, castor oil20–30 min × 7 daysIrritable bowel, digestive weakness, intestinal gas, umbilical hernia (early stage)
1
Preparation — Knead black gram (urad dal) flour with warm water into thick dough. Shape into ring/well (4–5 cm high, 1 cm thick walls) and seal firmly over target area with water to prevent leakage.
2
Fill & Maintain — Pour warm medicated oil (40–42°C) to fill the well. Replace oil when it cools using a syringe or ladle — maintain therapeutic temperature throughout. Monitor patient comfort and skin response.
3
Remove & Follow-up — Remove dough ring. Gentle massage with remaining oil into skin. Then apply warm fomentation for 5–10 min. Document treatment response. Advise gentle movement post-treatment — no cold exposure.
🟡 Kati Basti Evidence & Clinical Notes
Kati Basti for CLBP: 2 RCTs show significant pain reduction (VAS ↓ 55–65%) and improved Roland-Morris disability scores after 14 sessions. Mechanism: warm medicated oil penetrates peri-articular tissue, reduces inflammatory mediators locally, improves disc hydration, and modulates paraspinal muscle tone via thermal and pharmacological effects. Contraindications for all Basti treatments: open wounds over site, active skin infection, pregnancy (Kati/Hridaya), fever, severe osteoporosis, known oil allergy.
